LESSON  F.8
Lesson F.8 — Interactive

Connector Types & Mate/Demate

Read the harness drawing first. Always. Every connector on a spacecraft has a specific type, a specific orientation, and a specific place it belongs -- getting it wrong can damage flight hardware.

Real hardware — explore the photo

Tap the numbered markers to identify what you are looking at.
REAL HARDWARE

A MIL-DTL-38999 circular connector pair

A D38999/26TJ19SN circular connector pair: wall-mount receptacle with pins on the left, cable plug with sockets on the right. Tap the numbered markers to explore the details that matter during mate/demate.

Photo: Abaillieul, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons

Connector types

Click each component to see what it does and what ATLO verifies.

The rectangular family

REAL HARDWARE

The rectangular family: D-sub connectors

A 9-position D-sub connector with its socket positions numbered. Flight Micro-D and high-density D-sub connectors follow this same layout at much smaller pitch -- and the same rule applies: every numbered position lands exactly where the harness drawing says.

Photo: FireEmerald, CC BY-SA 4.0, via Wikimedia Commons

Why Connector Knowledge Matters

Mating and demating connectors is one of the most frequent physical tasks an ATLO engineer performs. Getting it wrong -- forcing a connector, mismatching pins, or applying incorrect torque -- can bend pins, damage sockets, or create an intermittent connection that causes an anomaly much later in the campaign.

Mate/Demate Discipline

Before mating any connector, verify you have the correct mating pair -- connector keying prevents most mismatches, but not all. Align connectors straight, never at an angle. Never force a connector. If it does not mate smoothly, stop and investigate before applying more force.

KEY POINT

CSTM and ISTM checks exist specifically because connector mating is a point where mistakes are common and consequences are real. Verify before you mate. Every time.
